1: The beginning of Zedekiah's reign — the textual note in brackets in many translations (the MT has 'Jehoiakim' but the chapter clearly concerns Zedekiah, a scribal error corrected in most manuscripts). The yoke sign-act is placed in a critical early moment of Zedekiah's reign.
2: עֲשֵׂה לְךָ מֹוסֵרוֹת וּמֹטוֹת (aseh lekha moserot umottot, make for yourself bonds and yoke-bars): the yoke-components — moserot are the leather straps/thongs that secure the yoke to the animal's neck; mottot are the wooden crossbars of the yoke. The complete physical apparatus of agricultural servitude becomes a walking prophetic symbol.
3: Send the yoke to the ambassadors of Edom, Moab, Ammon, Tyre, and Sidon — the nations whose envoys were gathered in Jerusalem, presumably forming a coalition against Babylon. Jeremiah disrupts the diplomatic conference with YHWH's counterword: submit, do not resist.
4: The message to their masters: This is what YHWH of armies, the God of Israel, says to you — the universal divine title. The nations are addressed as subjects of YHWH's word even though they are not Israel's covenant partners; YHWH's sovereignty is total.
5: אָנֹכִי עָשִׂיתִי אֶת-הָאָרֶץ (anokhi asiti et-ha'aretz, I made the earth): the creation sovereignty claim grounds the political claim. Because YHWH made the earth, the people, and the animals by his great power and outstretched arm (zeroa netuyah), he has the absolute right to assign ownership to whoever seems good to him (lakol asher yashar be'einav). The political theology of sovereignty: Creator = Owner = Sovereign over all political arrangements.
6: עַבְדִּי (avdi, my servant): Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon is again called YHWH's servant — the title appears twice (here and ch Jeremiah 25:9). Even the wild animals (chayyot hassadeh) are given to him to serve him. The cosmic servitude of nature to YHWH's appointed ruler is a creation-theology claim.
7: All nations (kol-hagoyim) will serve him — his son and his grandson — until the time (ad bo et yomo, until his time comes): a bounded sovereignty. The Babylonian empire is not permanent but has a YHWH-appointed duration. Then many nations and great kings will make him serve them — Babylon will be enslaved as it enslaved others.
8: The nation or kingdom that does not serve Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon and does not put its neck under his yoke (mottoh) — I will punish with sword, famine, and pestilence (cherev, ra'av, vaDever). Resistance to YHWH's appointed instrument is resistance to YHWH himself; the covenant-curse triad returns.
9: אַל-תִּשְׁמְעוּ אֶל-נְבִיאֵיכֶם (al-tishme'u el-nevieyekhem, do not listen to your prophets): followed by a list of false guidance-sources — קֹסְמֵיכֶם (qosmeykhem, your diviners), חֹלְמֵיכֶם (cholmeykhem, your dreamers), עֹנְנֵיכֶם (onneykhem, your soothsayers/cloud-readers), כַּשָּׁפֵיכֶם (kashafeykhem, your sorcerers). The full spectrum of divination practices condemned in Deuteronomy 18:10-12. These are the sources that say: 'You will not serve the king of Babylon' — but they lie.
10: They prophesy a lie (sheqer) to you — the false prophets promise what the people want to hear. The outcome of following false prophecy: removal (harchiqenu) from your land and expulsion in exile and perishing. Ironically, the prophecies meant to give security produce the catastrophe they promised to prevent.
11: But the nation that puts its neck under the yoke of the king of Babylon and serves him — I will leave it in its own land (vehinachtihu al-admatoh). The paradox of submission-as-survival: accepting the yoke is the path to staying in the land; resisting it leads to exile. This theological logic is exactly the kind of counter-intuitive message that got prophets killed.
12: Jeremiah speaks the same words to Zedekiah king of Judah: bring your necks under the yoke of the king of Babylon, serve him and his people, and you will live. The personal application of the prophetic word to the king — the message is not general but specific to the moment of decision Zedekiah faces.
13: Why should you and your people die by sword, famine, and pestilence (cherev vera'av vedaver) as YHWH has said about the nation that will not serve the king of Babylon? The rhetorical question exposes the self-destructiveness of the false-prophecy choice.
14: אַל-תִּשְׁמְעוּ אֶל-דִּבְרֵי הַנְּבִיאִים (al-tishme'u el-divrei haneviim, do not listen to the words of the prophets): the false prophets tell you that you will not serve the king of Babylon — שֶׁקֶר הֵם נִבְּאִים לָכֶם (sheqer hem nib'im lakhem, they are prophesying falsehood to you).
15: לֹא שְׁלַחְתִּים (lo shelachtim, I have not sent them): the diagnostic of false prophecy — the false prophet cannot demonstrate a divine commission. They prophesy in YHWH's name (bishmi) without YHWH's authorization. The result: I will drive you out and you will perish — both the prophets and those who believe them.
16: Jeremiah speaks to the priests and all the people: do not listen to the prophets who say the vessels of YHWH's house (kelei beit-YHWH) will soon be brought back from Babylon. שֶׁקֶר הֵם נִבְּאִים לָכֶם (sheqer hem nib'im lakhem, they are prophesying falsehood to you) — the repeated verdict.
17: Do not listen to them — serve the king of Babylon and live (ichyu). Why should this city become a desolation (chorbah)? The logic of prophetic pragmatism: not theological surrender but realistic assessment of what YHWH has determined.
18: But if they are prophets and if the word of YHWH is with them, let them intercede (yifge'u na) with YHWH of armies that the vessels remaining in the house of YHWH not go to Babylon — a challenge to the false prophets. If they have genuine prophetic access to YHWH, let them pray effectively. Their inability to prevent the remaining vessels from being taken proves their falsehood.
19: The specific vessels remaining — the bronze pillars (ha'amudim), the sea (hayyam), the stands (hamkhaonot), and the other vessels: the great ceremonial objects of the Solomonic temple. Their fate is being contested between true and false prophecy.
20: Nebuchadnezzar king of Babylon did not take them when he took Jeconiah son of Jehoiakim of Judah into exile from Jerusalem to Babylon — a historical note. The first deportation (597 BCE) included people but not all the temple vessels.
21: Thus says YHWH of armies, the God of Israel, concerning the vessels remaining in the house of YHWH, in the house of the king of Judah, and in Jerusalem — YHWH addresses specifically the objects of false prophecy's hopeful claims.
22: בָּבֶלָה יוּבָאוּ וְשָׁמָּה יִהְיוּ (Vavelah yuba'u veshammah yihyu, they will be brought to Babylon and there they will remain): YHWH's counter-prophecy to the false prophets. The vessels will go to Babylon. But: עַד יוֹם פָּקְדִי אֹתָם (ad yom poqdi otam, until the day I attend to them) — a bounded removal, not permanent loss. YHWH will bring them back — the same pattern as the exile of people: bounded by YHWH's purposes, not open-ended. Cf. Ezra 1:7-11 (Cyrus restores the temple vessels).
